key_genes_proteins: Cbh (bile salt hydrolase from Clostridium perfringens)
key_pathways: Bile salt metabolism (synthesis, conjugation, deconjugation,7α-dehydroxylation in the gut)
C. difficile spore germination pathway (triggered by taurocholate and glycocholate)
Sialic acid catabolism/regulation pathway (as proxy for dysbiosis)
key_metabolites: Taurocholate (conjugated primary bile salt, potent germinant of C. difficile)
Glycocholate (conjugated primary bile salt, germinant of C. difficile)
Cholate (unconjugated primary bile salt, inhibits C. difficile germination and growth)
Deoxycholate (secondary bile acid, derivative of cholate)
Sialic acid (proxy signal for antibiotic-induced dysbiosis)

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Bile salt hydrolase (Cbh) deconjugates conjugated bile salts (taurocholate, glycocholate) into unconjugated forms (cholate), reducing C. difficile spore germination and vegetative growth.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Purified Cbh converted99.2% taurocholate to cholate (Fig.2C); Cbh treatment reduced endospore germination by up to81% and viable vegetative cells by up to82% (Fig.2A, B). Recombinant Cbh in probiotics achieved98% reduction in spore germination (Fig.4B) and strong reduction in viable vegetative cells (Fig.4C).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Sialic acid serves as a proxy signal for antibiotic-induced microbiome dysbiosis, and a genetically encoded sensor (pNanA-NanR) detects sialic acid to dynamically regulate Cbh expression.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Sialic acid levels increased6-fold upon antibiotic treatment in mice (Fig.7B). The sensor (pNanA-NanR) was responsive to sialic acid (Fig.3A) and dynamically regulated GFP expression (Supplementary Fig.2C, D). The sensor-amplifier-actuator circuit (EcN-Cbh) showed enhanced Cbh expression with sialic acid induction (Fig.3F, G).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Engineered probiotics (EcN-Cbh) restore intestinal bile salt metabolism, decreasing taurocholate and increasing cholate levels, thereby inhibiting C. difficile infection in vivo.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: In mice, EcN-Cbh reduced taurocholate and increased cholate levels in feces (Fig.7C, D). EcN-Cbh led to100% survival, least weight loss, lowest clinical symptom score, and decreased C. difficile abundance (Fig.5D–F, Fig.6A). Histological injury scores were lower in EcN-Cbh-treated mice (Fig.6C).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: The deconjugated product cholate directly inhibits growth of vegetative C. difficile cells.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Growth inhibition was attributed to cholate (Supplementary Fig.3B, C). Coculture assays showed that EcN-Cbh preincubated with taurocholate significantly reduced viable vegetative C. difficile CFUs (Fig.4C).

key_findings: Bile salt hydrolase Cbh deconjugates taurocholate and glycocholate to cholate, strongly inhibiting C. difficile spore germination and vegetative growth in vitro.
A sialic acid-responsive genetic circuit (sensor-amplifier-actuator) in probiotic E. coli Nissle1917 dynamically regulates Cbh expression in response to antibiotic-induced dysbiosis.
Engineered probiotics (EcN-Cbh) achieved100% survival rate in a murine CDI model, with improved clinical symptoms, reduced weight loss, and decreased C. difficile abundance.
EcN-Cbh treatment significantly increased cholate levels and decreased taurocholate levels in the mouse gut, correlating with infection protection.
The complete sensor-amplifier-actuator circuit was required for optimal protection; constitutive Cbh expression (no sensor) led to lower survival despite comparable bile salt conversion.
EcN-Cbh reduced C. difficile toxin (TcdA) levels and improved viability of human epithelial cells (Caco-2) exposed to C. difficile supernatants.
The engineered probiotics improved microbiome diversity (Shannon index) in infected mice.
Histopathological assessment showed that EcN-Cbh alleviated colonic tissue damage (reduced submucosal edema and neutrophil infiltration).
relevance_to_research_topic: This paper provides mechanistic insights into how modulation of intestinal bile salt metabolism (a key metabolite pathway) can influence C. difficile infection (CDI). While the research topic specifically focuses on high-iron diet effects, this paper establishes a direct link between microbial metabolite profiles (bile salts) and CDI severity, and demonstrates that altering the bile salt composition (e.g., increasing cholate, decreasing taurocholate) can inhibit C. difficile. The concept of 'diet–microbiome–host' interaction is exemplified by the use of sialic acid as a dysbiosis signal. Although iron is not studied, the paper's findings on bile salt dynamics and engineered probiotics provide a framework for investigating how dietary components like iron might similarly alter the gut metabolome and affect CDI.
